title: The CIA Scientist Who Built UFOs Before Bob Lazar (Townsend Brown Documentary)
summary: This documentary-style interview explores the life and work of Thomas Townsend Brown, a mid-century American inventor who allegedly discovered a link between electromagnetism and gravity through his 'Biefeld-Brown effect.' The episode argues that Brown's classified work influenced black aerospace programs, potentially including the B2 stealth bomber, and suggests his story provides context for understanding later UFO-related claims like those of Bob Lazar.

acronyms.definition: US intelligence agency allegedly involved in anti-gravity research and UFO secrecy programs
CIA division involved in theoretical anti-gravity research
Organization founded by Tom DeLonge involved in UFO research and disclosure

organizations_mentioned: University of North Carolina Chapel Hill
American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics

events.description: Thomas Townsend Brown was born in Zanesville, Ohio
Brown went into Nazi Germany to retrieve exotic propulsion technology
Brown's work was compromised by a Soviet spy acting as a janitor during a Pearl Harbor demonstration
Major General Victor Gruhn witnessed Brown's gravitator demonstration at the Townsend Brown Foundation
Brown's Winter Haven proposal was created, allegedly resembling America's secret UFO program structure
Jacques Cornillon facilitated Brown's vacuum experiments at the Montgolfier facility in Paris
Agnew Bahnson held the Chapel Hill conference bringing together top theoretical physicists to study gravity, sponsored by Wright Airfield
Brown shuts down Guidance Technologies with no explanation
Northrop writes and then retracts a paper on electro-aerodynamics
Australian intelligence memo documents US government anti-gravity research programs
F-117 stealth fighter crashed in Bakersfield
John Lear leaks story about F-117 stealth fighter to George Knapp's news station; Aurora appears as line item in Congressional budget with $2.3 billion
Bob Lazar goes public with his Area 51 story; Aurora spotted by oil rig engineer near North Sea
Air Force tested Biefeld-Brown experiment in vacuum using only 19 kilovolts
NASA announced it would be ending breakthrough physics and gravity manipulation efforts
NASA Marshall Space Flight Center files patent for asymmetrical capacitor under engineer John Campbell
Paul Schatzkin met Jacques Cornillon in Pennsylvania before he died at age 98
Robert Weiss of Skunk Works connected with Tom DeLonge and TTSA
MIT demonstrated ion plane based on Townsend Brown's research

key_claims.claim: Robert Sarbacher admitted to UFO researcher William Steinman that rumors surrounding UFO crashes are substantially correct
David Grusch publicly stated that Robert Sarbacher helped set up UFO secrecy
The Biefeld-Brown effect produces thrust from negative to positive electrodes even in a vacuum, far greater than ionic wind can explain
Jacques Cornillon confirmed the anomalous effect of Brown's experiments in vacuum at age 98
Edward Teller witnessed Brown's experiment and said he didn't understand how it works
Agnew Bahnson and his Institute of Field Physics at UNC Chapel Hill were academic satellites of the CIA studying anti-gravity
The 1957 Chapel Hill conference sent academic physics into a dead end by establishing quantum gravity leading to string theory
The B2 stealth bomber uses an electrostatic effect in its wings producing a Biefeld-Brown effect
Bill Gunston wrote that the B2 would be incapable of its credited performance without some electrogravitic propulsion boost
Brown intentionally showed people wrong versions of his work (wounded prairie chicken routine) after Soviet spy compromise in 1950
A 1943 FBI file states Brown knew more about radar detection than any individual in the US Navy
John Lear was a CIA pilot who introduced Bob Lazar to George Knapp
Lewis Witten from Martin Corporation stated that Townsend discovered a type of bismuth that was repelled instead of attracted
Bill Lear's headquarters were across the street from Townsend Brown's Guidance Technologies and they had lunch practically every day
